blocks.content: Marcus didn't come down for dinner.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: Should someone check on him?

blocks.speaker: Nadia
blocks.content: He said he needed rest. Let him sleep.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: I'll save him a plate.

They ate without him. The conversation was easy — the second-night looseness of people who had gotten the first-night performance out of their system. Cade told a story about his landlord. Dean argued with Sophie about something small and harmless. Wren refilled her water and didn't say much and nobody expected her to.
At nine-seventeen, the ceiling came alive.
Muffled — the way voices are through floor joists and plaster — but unmistakable. Marcus's voice. Another voice underneath it, harder to make out. Back and forth. Heated.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Work call.

blocks.speaker: Sophie
blocks.content: On a Saturday night?

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: You know how he is. If a client calls, he picks up.

They listened for another moment. The voices continued — tense, indistinct. Then Cade topped off his drink and changed the subject and they let Marcus have his privacy the way you let someone you love have their privacy, without thinking twice about it.
The voices stopped at nine forty-three.
By ten-thirty, when the dishes were done and the wine was finished and the conversation had run its natural course, Marcus still hadn't come down.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: I'm going to knock. It's weird that he hasn't come down.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: He's probably just asleep.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Then I'll knock quietly.
He went upstairs. He knocked. Nothing. He knocked again — louder this time — and called Marcus's name through the door.
Nothing.
He tried the handle. Locked.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Dean.
His voice carried down the stairs in a way that brought everyone up.
Dean knocked. Harder. Called his name. Sophie called it too. Wren stood at the end of the hallway and said nothing, watching the door with an expression nobody had seen on her before.
Two minutes of this. Then five. The kind of minutes that change the texture of a night completely.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: Marcus. Marcus, open the door. I need you to open the door right now.
Silence from the other side.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Break it.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: Cade—

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Dean. Break it.
Dean put his shoulder into the door once. The frame held. Twice.
The door opened.
The cold hit them first.
